blueprint_refs: Blueprint: "treat these as the resident's Pattern/Persona representations" and "STRICTLY pre-target when generating targets"
Blueprint: "Motivation for a target day is computed from the past 7 observed days prior to the target date"

evidence.metrics: Validation metrics are decent but non-trivial errors remain (stop_count_abs_mean_error=0.388, stop_count_kl=0.224, tod_jsd_avg=0.0275, trip_distance_wasserstein=0.364), consistent with using a single per-user pattern fit from all training days rather than recomputing per target day using only days < target.

missing-per-user-pretarget-conditioning-.error_identification: Pattern/anchors are fit once per user from the whole training split, then reused for every validation target day. Blueprint requires Pattern/Persona/Motivation to be computed strictly from days before each target date (no future leakage within 2021).
missing-per-user-pretarget-conditioning-.root_cause_analysis: In main(), patterns are fit from split.train_by_user[uid] once and reused; there is no per-target recomputation using only training days earlier than each validation date. This violates the per-target preconditioning requirement and can bias start-time/stop-count/transition distributions for later validation dates.
missing-per-user-pretarget-conditioning-.correct_approach: For each (user, validation_day) during rollout, derive an effective training subset = {d in 2021 | d < validation_day} (and optionally also within the user's training portion) and fit/refresh the agent’s Pattern (anchors, histograms, transitions) from that subset before generating that day. Cache by (user, cutoff_day) or by validation day to avoid recomputing from scratch. Ensure Motivation uses only the prior 7 observed days < target_day (already enforced) and that Pattern uses only < target_day too.
missing-per-user-pretarget-conditioning-.key_insight: When the spec says "strictly pre-target", treat Pattern/Persona as time-indexed state: either recompute per target cutoff or maintain incremental sufficient statistics; don’t reuse a single fit across multiple target dates.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint: "modeling in minutes" and "timestamps are rounded to nearest minute for fitting and generation"
Blueprint policy: "time-of-day activity propensity" and timing conditioned on Pattern/Persona/Motivation

evidence.metrics: tod_jsd_avg=0.0275 suggests remaining mismatch in arrival time distribution; model uses coarse 15-minute bin profiles and a simple start-time histogram without a learned within-day intensity process.

missing-minute-level-time-modeling-uses-.error_identification: Time-of-day matching is only indirectly controlled (15-min global category profile + per-user start-time histogram + lognormal dwell). This can leave systematic ToD divergence even when category/transition distributions look good.
missing-minute-level-time-modeling-uses-.root_cause_analysis: The simulator does not fit a per-user or global arrival-time hazard/intensity over minutes; it uses a coarse profile at 15-minute bins and then advances time by dwell+travel. This limits the model’s ability to reproduce multi-peak daily rhythms and late-night/early-morning structure.
missing-minute-level-time-modeling-uses-.correct_approach: Keep minute resolution, but replace or augment the 15-min profile with (a) per-user (or persona) arrival intensity over finer bins (e.g., 5 minutes) and/or (b) a learned inter-event time distribution conditioned on category and time-of-day. Concretely: estimate empirical inter-arrival gaps by category and time bin from training; sample next gap from that conditional distribution rather than only dwell+travel. Maintain monotonic time constraint.
missing-minute-level-time-modeling-uses-.key_insight: To match ToD distributions, controlling only start-time + dwell is often insufficient; add an explicit arrival-time or inter-arrival model conditioned on time-of-day.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint: "trip distance distributions ... join lat/lon via poi_category_192021_longitude_latitude.json"

evidence.metrics: trip_distance_wasserstein=0.3639; evaluator meta shows coord_match_rate gt=0.3010 sim=0.2975, and load logs report 3858 trajectory-only POIs without coordinates were added.

trip-distance-eval-sensitive-to-missing-.error_identification: Trip-distance metric is computed only on legs where both POIs have coordinates; the pipeline injects many coordinate-missing POIs and also uses distance fallbacks when coords are missing, which can distort both behavior and evaluation comparability.
trip-distance-eval-sensitive-to-missing-.root_cause_analysis: In load_data(), any POI token seen in trajectories but missing from the POI file is inserted with lat/lon=None, expanding candidate pools with coord-missing POIs. In _choose_next_poi and _travel_minutes, missing distances are replaced by a small fallback (capped by 5km), which compresses simulated distance variability and can bias the Wasserstein distance even if coord_match_rate is similar.
trip-distance-eval-sensitive-to-missing-.correct_approach: Separate concerns: (1) keep missing-coord POIs for output validity, but (2) when the metric conditions on coord-known legs, explicitly prefer coord-known POIs for non-anchor moves when current/anchor has coords (already partially done) and (3) raise the fallback distance distribution to match observed coord-known distance statistics (e.g., sample fallback distances from the empirical gt distance distribution conditional on category/time rather than a fixed <=5km cap). Additionally, report and optionally optimize on coord_match_rate in calibration meta (without inventing a new official metric).
trip-distance-eval-sensitive-to-missing-.key_insight: If evaluation conditions on data availability (coords), your simulator must (a) control the missingness mechanism and (b) make fallback values distributional, not constant, or you will bias distance metrics.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint: "return-home hazard params" and "Calibrate to match distribution of last-visit times"

evidence.metrics: tod_jsd_avg=0.0275 and stop_count metrics (abs_mean_error=0.388, kl=0.224) can be impacted by an overly simplistic end-of-day/home-return process; sample outputs show frequent very-late activities and sometimes multiple late-night stops.

return-home-hazard-not-calibrated-to-las.error_identification: The model’s end-of-day behavior is governed by a simple late-time linear factor and a single bias; this may not match observed last-visit-time distributions and can push stop counts and ToD tails off-target.
return-home-hazard-not-calibrated-to-las.root_cause_analysis: generate_day() adds an optional home return only if simulated visits < stop_count, with p_home based on (t-18:00) and return_home_bias. There is no calibrated hazard over time that can end the day early, nor is there a last-activity-time objective term; calibration therefore may fit stop counts but miss last-visit timing structure.
return-home-hazard-not-calibrated-to-las.correct_approach: Implement an explicit per-step stopping/return-home hazard that depends on current time (and optionally cumulative fatigue) and can terminate generation before reaching the planned stop_count, then fit its parameters using training last-visit-time statistics (or include ToD tail penalties indirectly). Keep blueprint constraint that Motivation can modulate this hazard.
return-home-hazard-not-calibrated-to-las.key_insight: To match ToD tails and realistic day endings, you need a stopping process; a single post-hoc ‘maybe return home’ rule is usually insufficient.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint: "Calibrate ... on 2021 training period" and "Validate on holdout dates"

evidence.metrics: Calibration best objective on training subsample=0.7073 vs validation objective=0.9076; calibration iter objectives vary widely (e.g., 0.707 to 8.04), indicating a noisy optimization surface even with rng reset.

calibration-objective-mismatch-training-.error_identification: Calibration uses a 250-day subsample and random-search parameters; large objective swings suggest sensitivity to parameter proposals and dataset subsampling, and the best-on-subsample parameters do not fully transfer to validation.
calibration-objective-mismatch-training-.root_cause_analysis: The calibrator optimizes on a fixed-size random subsample of training days and uses a simple random search without stratification by user/date or multiple rollouts per parameter set. This can overfit the subsample and yield high-variance objective estimates.
calibration-objective-mismatch-training-.correct_approach: Stabilize calibration signal: (1) stratify subsampling across users and across time (early/late 2021), (2) evaluate each proposed parameter set on multiple disjoint subsamples or bootstrap splits and average the objective, (3) optionally narrow parameter ranges (e.g., travel_time_scale) based on data-derived priors to reduce variance.
calibration-objective-mismatch-training-.key_insight: If the optimizer is random-search and the rollout is stochastic, reduce variance first (stratified subsamples + replicate evaluations) before increasing search complexity.

evidence.metrics: Execution succeeded in this run, but the script includes OpenAI API calls and env requirements that are unrelated to the mobility simulation pipeline and could fail in other environments.

nonessential-openai-dependency-in-core-s.error_identification: The file contains unrelated OpenAI/LLM code paths that can raise environment/import errors if accidentally triggered and adds maintenance risk.
nonessential-openai-dependency-in-core-s.root_cause_analysis: OpenAI helper functions and prompt builders are present at top-level in the same script as the simulator, despite not being used by main(). This is dead code for the blueprint and can cause confusion and potential runtime failures if called.
nonessential-openai-dependency-in-core-s.correct_approach: Remove the unused OpenAI helper block from this simulation script (or gate it behind a CLI flag and ensure it is never imported/executed in the default run). Keep the simulator self-contained and data-driven per blueprint.
nonessential-openai-dependency-in-core-s.key_insight: Don’t ship unrelated optional dependencies inside the core execution path; isolate optional tooling to avoid accidental failures and simplify reproducibility.
